speaker: interviewee
text: All right. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: --now we're in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a baby. And then I had another baby. And then I had another one and another one. So I have

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the names of your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son Richard, he was born June 24, 1954. He is married, now, of course, and has children of his own. A set of twins, Ethan and Matthew, and they're now 6 and 1/2. In 1956, I gave birth to my daughter Anita. She was born August 29. And she too is married. She is a director of B.C. Hydro. By the way, Richard is an architect. And his wife Carla, she's a lawyer. Great, great people. My daughter is absolutely fabulous. And she's married to David. And they have a little boy, Michael. And Michael was two July the 15th, just recently. In 1956, July the second, my third child, my son Jeffrey, was born. Jeffrey is a mathematician, or graduated in mathematics. And he's married to Wendy. And they have three children. They have Jason, who is-- who was born March 21, 1990. And in '91, I think, came along Emily. And in '96, came along Noah. And in-- on April 5, 1963, my daughter Lisa was born. And Lisa is now married to Larry Rothman. And they have a little boy called Jake, who's as red as they come and has the most smiling face you have ever seen. And I'm just lucky, lucky, lucky lady in that respect. I have eight wonderful children and seven grandchildren. The sad part is that I lost my mother just this year. She died January the 18th, 1996. But I have my dad. And I have a good support system. I've got wonderful friends. Not very many, but the ones that I have are salt of the earth. And I work. I've done a lot of things in my time in Canada, from selling shirts and ties to managing the first salon at Holt Renfrew in Vancouver, to doing catering, to being an importer of costume jewelry. And then I went back to school. And now I work for the Lodzer Centre Holocaust Congregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back over events that we've heard about, how do you feel they've directed your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] One does not reach now, here, without having to take a long journey from there and then. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] For a long time, I did not want to be identified. I wanted to push it away. I didn't want to make it me and mine. It took awhile. I have volunteered in many areas, member of Hadassah to help Israel-- for Israel, I always worked. And in the beginning, I was losing the language. So my-- my contact with the language was through records. And then, of course, I met people from Israel and went to Israel many times, and recaptured this wonderful language. And there is a feeling of belonging that is so great, and such a comfort, that you work for that. I don't often allow myself to think what has happened to the warriors. They sadden me terribly. You know, one never knows what one's life would have been like. The opportunities, the chances-- maybe not the chances. And what has happened to the people that were in my family. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We never-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we're left with just a handful. Can you imagine? My mother had nine brothers and sisters. My father, five. Just from that branch, how many people would be mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- now, my story is not a horrendous one. Surviving, how we survived-- probably my parents would have been able to tell you more meaningful things, because they were adult. And they knew a difference. I didn't know-- you can only tell how bad things are when you are able to compare. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I couldn't. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I have heard stories. And these are my people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has it affected your view of human nature, of-- of people generally?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a long time, I didn't want to be together with those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those people being?

speaker: interviewee
text: People who could have orchestrated such a Holocaust. Such a life. And people, individuals, also, who did not extend a helping hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mean people in Poland? or Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean Russia, Poland, Germany, even Canada. Even some among our own. It was incredible, really incredible. When we came to Canada in '49, it's not the same as people immigrate now to Canada. There was no assistance of any kind that you can see now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your education is provided. Housing is provided. Health care is provided. There is a Jewish community to go into who view the survivors in a different light than what they did then. You know? What did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came, it-- a, it was very difficult to find a place to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: With children, with children you weren't allowed, you know, that-- that-- they didn't want to rent. You were the outsider again. There was no relationship, co-relationship between Jews that came, were fortunate enough to come to Canada before the war, and those who have survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't feel a part of the Jewish community here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt only a Jewish community of the Jews who came to Toronto after the war. Survivors. And they congregated together. If you were to go on-- for a walk between Bathurst and Spadina on a Sunday afternoon, you would meet everybody that you knew in-- in Toronto, who came to-- to here. Nobody had telephones. You couldn't use a telephone for if you rented the flat. And they-- they perpetuated their way of life before the war, where you went for a promenade and you met people. And you stopped in. And you didn't call to say, come on Tuesday afternoon at 3 o'clock for a cup of coffee. If you wanted to see your friend, you popped in to see your friend. And it was just fine. That was fine. You didn't have to make any special arrangements. Now, if you tell anybody, why don't you come by, it's not an invitation. Invitation is specific time, specific day, you-- you prepare, you-- it's a different way. It's a different way. One is not better or worse than the other. But this is how life is.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the people-- and I have seen it in my parents. My parents and I belong to the Workmen's Circle. There is a branch made up specifically of the new immigrants to Canada. They, too, were not integrated into the Workmen's Circle. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp Yungvelt-- or rather Naivelt-- or Yungvelt, sorry. Naivelt is someplace else. Camp Yungvelt was part of the Workmen's Circle. It was a colony where they have cottages. Well, in those days, the colony belonged to prominent Workmen's Circle people who were here from a long time. So among our own, there was also this-- this difference that you could see.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you think of people watching this tape in the future-- perhaps your grandchildren, great-grandchildren-- what would you hope they would learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've thought about it a long time. Not just for this interview. There was a point in time where I wasn't feeling well and I wanted to be sure, what would I like to leave for my grandchildren. Don't mean a Persian carpet or a diamond. But what, you know, that when I'm no longer here, they would think or relate-- you know, what could I tell them to-- of some significance that they would-- and there are only very few things. Number one, love one another, help one another, be bonded. That's important. Seek people who are people, for the sake what's in their heart, not what's in their pocket. And help wherever you can. I think they'll have rich lives if they do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Well, that's me. Must have been, I don't know, maybe a year and a half. It would have been 1938. And that would have been in Warsaw. Yeah. I had blond hair. This picture was taken in the summer of 1939. That was just before the war. But in the summertime we went to the country in [NON-ENGLISH]. On the left and on the right of me are my mother's cousins, the ones that we stayed with just before the-- when we first came to [NON-ENGLISH] after the bombing. And the man standing is the husband of the one on the left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed with them. That is me when we first came to Germany after the war. And as you can see, I still had the-- my long braids, and my mother's pride and joy. I was the only one in [NON-ENGLISH], in Russia, with long hair. And my mother took excellent care of my hair. Because if anyone found lice, there were shaven right in the middle of the head. So I kept my long hair and they could smell me coming with the kerosene right through my school days in Russia. But I kept my long hair and my mother's pride. That's a group picture of my Hashomer Hatzair days in-- in Germany. I am on the top row with the hair-- sort of a crown in the hair. And at the bottom, there's my teacher, one of the teachers. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember her name. Rachel, I think it was, Rachel. And the one next to her was [NON-ENGLISH] from Israel, who came to see how the school was going. And the rest of them are all-- and I am just to the left of the boy in the middle row, on top of-- you know, on the top row.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're to the left of the Magen David.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're somewhere. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you where, you know, if I had a closer look. But this is my whole Hashomer Hatzair group in [NON-ENGLISH]. And this picture was taken in the beginning or the spring of 1948, with all the-- the whole camp. I am in there someplace. Just exactly where from this distance I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the group in which you were a leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was a leader of the very youngest. My mother thought it was very great. But when we had meetings in the evenings, she came-- she would come running and she-- she embarrassed me so by saying, how could you keep our children at this hour? And I thought I was so grown up. May 14, 1948. The very, very first Yom Ha'atzmaut. And that's my mother and I. This picture was also taken at the same time, in 1948. That's, again, one of the [NON-ENGLISH] that came. I am on the left. And on the right is Cypora. And Cypora's uncle was the one who said he knew me when he visited [NON-ENGLISH], when he saw Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who actually identified--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who identified me in-- from the picture that my father showed him. That was her uncle, Cypora's uncle. This picture was taken in Russia, my mother and I. From-- this picture is of my mother and I. And then at the same time was taken another picture, just I, that I sent to my father and that he had shown to that man who identified me when we came from Germany. And this must have been taken in 1945, probably. This photo was taken in 1949, just before we came to Canada. And that's my father, rejoined us, my mother, and I. That's my son Richard and his wife Carla. This was taken at the wedding of his sister, Anita. So they were attendants. Those are their sons. Matthew is on the left. Ethan is on the right. They are now 6 and 1/2. And they are the children of Richard and Carla. That's my daughter Anita and her husband, David. That was taken when they got married. That would have been in 1992. They both live in BC, in Vancouver. Quite-- That is Michael Alexander Wetton, Anita's little boy. He is two. What a smiling face. He is-- too bad that he is so far away. That's my son Jeffrey and his wife, Wendy. That, too, was taken the day of Anita's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandchildren, Jason and Emily. And they belong to Jeffrey and Wendy. Jason is now six. Emily is five. That's my newest addition, Noah Harrison Wolfe. He's the newest addition to Wendy and Jeff as well, and a brother for Emily and Jason. As you can see, the-- the frame gives you away what's his name, is there's Noah's ark and two-by-twos. And there is Noah. That's my daughter, Lisa, and Larry Rothman. They live in Las Vegas. And that, too, was taken the day of Anita's wedding. They came in for that occasion. They've been there quite some time now. That's my grandson, Jake Rothman, who lives in Las Vegas with his mommy and daddy, Lisa and Larry. This picture was taken at a pidyon haben of Jason, Wendy's and Jeffrey's son. And on the left is my father. On the right, my mother. The two young men hanging on my arm are my twin grandsons, Ethan and Matthew. And the one in my hand is Jason. The lady in between myself and my father is Mrs. Cameron. She helped me raise the children. She was with the family for 25 years. That's my mom and dad. This, too, was taken in 1992. And they look like a very happy pair. And from these two people, I haven't counted how many offsprings there are, how many issues. But there's-- four and seven is 11-- 12 people. They're responsible for 12 people, so far.
